patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I keep tripping over my feet and my ankles feel weak, and it’s getting worse.” Early on, volunteer: frequent tripping on curbs/stairs like your toes don’t lift, weak ankles with repeat sprains, higher arches/shoes not fitting, calves looking thinner, intermittent toe numbness/tingling. If asked, add: can’t run, fatigue with long walks, hand clumsiness (buttons/zippers) and weaker grip, burning/shooting foot pain at night, worse balance in dark/uneven ground, gradual progression over years, family history of “weak ankles/foot problems.” Don’t mention unless specifically asked about sensation/skin: reduced pain/temperature in feet, unnoticed blisters/sores, curling toes/pressure spots, activity avoidance from fear of falling, night cramps. Be reluctant/embarrassed about public falls, needing railings/walls, and sweaty/odorous feet; disclose only with gentle, direct questions. Share misconceptions: worry about Parkinson’s, pinched nerve, or bad shoes. If clinician is empathic, soften, admit fears about falls, work, and insurance coverage for genetic testing/PT/AFOs.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "hereditary spastic paraplegia" and confirms this is a chronic, typically slowly progressive upper motor neuron disorder causing leg spasticity and gait difficulty.

Acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty where appropriate and mentions key alternative diagnoses to consider/rule out (e.g., multiple sclerosis, cervical/thoracic myelopathy, ALS/PLS, B12 deficiency) without overcomplicating the L1 scenario.
Invites questions, checks understanding (teach-back), and provides a basic follow-up plan or next steps even if not using a follow-up tool.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and links it to the patient’s reported sleep complaint.

Screens for common contributors/secondary causes: stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, nocturia, GERD, restless legs symptoms, snoring/witnessed apneas, substance/caffeine/alcohol/nicotine use, and medication contributors (e.g., stimulants, steroids, decongestants).
Elicits sleep history and habits: bedtime/wake time, time in bed, naps, sleep environment, screen use, shift work/jet lag, and variability across weekdays/weekends.

Explains treatment and includes both "CBT-I" as first-line and a balanced discussion of "sedative-hypnotics" as short-term/selected-use options with risks (next-day impairment, falls, dependence, complex sleep behaviors).
If the agent proposes any medication, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; failure to do so is a critical error.
Avoids prescribing controlled sedative-hypnotics as default first-line for uncomplicated insomnia; emphasizes nonpharmacologic management and shared decision-making.
